data.title: Pure Storage Is Too Expensive For My Liking
data.description:
One very interesting company that has really taken off in recent months is Pure Storage, Inc. (PSTG). To be honest with you, the first time I ever heard about the business, I thought that it was some sort of self-storage company. But it certainly does not fit under that category. In fact, it operates as a leader in data management and storage services. In my last article about the business, published in late August of this year, I went into more detail about this. But in short, it uses all flash functionality to help customers increase cloud native applications and to monetize data infrastructure. A big part of its mission right now is to help customers accelerate their AI adoption.

As exciting as this space is, in my previous article about the business, I called it a Sell candidate. This might seem surprising given how rapid the growth in the AI market is. However, even though the business itself is doing exceptionally well, shares looked very expensive. They were so expensive, in fact, that I did not believe that any realistic growth would justify meaningful upside. Since then, however, the stock has outperformed the market, jumping 23% while the S&P 500 (SP500) is up 5.5%. This seems to indicate that, for now at least, Mr. Market disagrees with me. But at the end of the day, I suspect that shares will underperform for an extended window of time.

It is very possible that I am wrong. Even though I don't think I am, as new data comes in, it is necessary to revisit the picture and evaluate what kind of situation investors are faced with. It just so happens that, on December 2nd, management will be announcing financial results for the third quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, there is a lot of optimism from both management and analysts. But unless management comes out with something really exceptional, I don't think that my overall assessment here will have been wrong. Because of that, I think that maintaining it as a Sell candidate is appropriate.

Checking In On Pure Storage

Before I get into what analysts anticipate for third quarter earnings, I do think it would be useful to touch on, just briefly, overall financial performance for the business in recent memory. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2023 through 2025 fiscal years. And in the chart below, you can see results for the first half of 2026 compared to the same time of 2025. I already covered these details in my prior article. But in short, the company is benefiting from significant user growth as demand for its services, as well as new product offerings, come about. By the second quarter of 2026, the company serviced 62% of Fortune 500 customers, with the total customer count exceeding 13,500. Back in the second quarter of 2023, it worked with only 56% of Fortune 500 firms, and serviced around 10,500 customers.

Overall, this trend is great to see. And management has done a great job. The current expectation that analysts have is that this performance, from a fundamental standpoint, will continue. For the third quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, for instance, they expect revenue of $956.5 million. That would be up 15.1% over the $831.1 million the company reported a year earlier. This might sound extraordinary. But it's important to know that they aren't the only ones who expect this kind of year over year sales increase. Management mentioned, when they announced results for the second quarter of 2026, that revenue would come in at between $950 million and $960 million. So, analysts are right around the midpoint of that range.

There's also the expectation that bottom-line results will improve also. The current belief that analysts have is that earnings per share will come in at $0.24. That's up nicely from the $0.19 per share reported the same time last year and would translate to a rise in net profits from $63.6 million to $82.4 million. On an adjusted basis, earnings per share are expected to total around $0.58. That would represent an improvement over the $0.50 per share reported a year ago and would mean a rise in adjusted net income from $171.4 million to $199.2 million.

It is noteworthy that management has not provided any estimates when it comes to earnings per share. They have, however, provided other profitability guidance. They anticipate, for instance, non-GAAP operating income for the quarter of between $185 million and $195 million. At the midpoint, this would be 13.6% above the $167.3 million the company reported for last year. Considering that the adjusted earnings per share estimates call for a rise of 16% compared to the same time last year, it is unlikely that analysts are off by much, if at all. In the table above, you can also see what certain cash flow metrics were for the company in the third quarter of 2025. It is highly likely that if revenue, earnings, and adjusted earnings, all increase year over year, that these cash flow metrics will also.

One thing that, I believe, would be particularly interesting will be data that management offers when it comes to its different offerings. Examples of offerings would be those that fall under the "product" category and the "subscription services" category. The product category includes things like physical hardware systems and one time software licenses that are sold to customers. Examples here include its FlashArray systems, FlashBlade systems, and its Portworx Enterprises licenses. Meanwhile, its subscription services category involves regular software updates and maintenance services, fully managed storage subscription offerings, and a wide array of other service offerings that bring in recurring and predictable revenue.

By management's own admission, they are continuing to shift in the direction of prioritizing subscription services. The stability in revenue and cash flows certainly is a contributor here. But there are other reasons behind this as well. In the chart above, you can see the overall product and subscription services revenues, gross profits, and gross profit margins that the company has achieved over the last three completed fiscal years and for the first half of 2026 compared to the same time of 2025.

As you can tell, the product side of the business has become increasingly challenged in recent years, particularly when it comes to profits. At the same time, however, management has been pushing to achieve greater subscription revenue, and with that growth has come higher margins. Take results for the first half of 2026 as an example. The margin difference between products and subscription services has become massive. If all of the revenue the company generated during the first half of the 2026 fiscal year came from subscription services, then total gross profit generated by the company would be $168.9 million more than if all of the revenue came from product sales.

The deliberate effort that management has made to make this transition is very quickly turning the subscription services side of the business into the real value driver for investors. From 2023 through 2024, product sales dropped from 65.1% of total sales down to 53.6%. By the first half of the 2026 fiscal year, they had finally dipped to 49.9%. Subscription services revenue, meanwhile, has taken the lead. Furthermore, subscription services have gone from accounting for only 35.6% of total gross profit for the company back in 2023 to 49.2% last year. And for the first half of the 2026 fiscal year, this increased further to 53.8%.

Investors should continue to monitor the companies transition from a product oriented one to a subscription services one. But that's not the only thing that they should keep an eye out for. Management has come out with multiple news items over the last couple of months. In late October, for instance, they announced what is essentially a bundled, validated architecture for enterprise scale AI deployment that covers multiple areas like compute, storage, networking, and software. This has been done in coordination with Cisco (CSCO) and Nvidia (NVDA), with the latter party’s technology validating this development. The objective on this front is to make the barrier to AI adoption lower for enterprises, meaning that costs should reduce and the time to fully adopt AI infrastructure contract.

There have been other noteworthy developments as well. In late September, as an example, management made public that it had made certain enhancements to its Pure Storage Platform that should, in theory, make it easier for corporations to use AI to the utmost. This involved the introduction of Pure Storage Cloud, which the company has positioned as an "Azure Native" offering that empowers customers to store managed data in public cloud environments while also helping to provide automation, governance, data mobility, and data management, no matter where the data happens to be. That includes on premise, in the cloud, or even in a hybrid environment.

These types of offerings pave the way for even more AI adoption as time goes on. And normally, this is something that I would be excited about. But unfortunately, the downside is that shares are expensive and getting more expensive by the day.

In the chart above, you can see how the stock is valued using estimates for the 2026 fiscal year and historical results for 2025. That chart also shows how the company was valued when I wrote about it earlier this year. To be clear, when it comes to the EBITDA, a big differentiator in it compared to adjusted operating cash flow has to do with stock-based compensation. I don't add that back in because it is a real expense at the end of the day, even if it's non-cash in nature. This, I believe, is a more accurate depiction of how expensive shares are since management would theoretically have to pay out that stock-based compensation in the form of cash if paying out in stock was not possible or was intensely undesirable. The adjusted operating cash flow, by comparison, ignores this.

Takeaway

Operationally speaking, Pure Storage is a fascinating business that's doing a phenomenal job. It is in an interesting phase of its life, with a continued transition away from products and toward subscription services. Margins should expand as this progresses. And right now, analysts and management are both optimistic about the future. I, on the other hand, am concerned about the price that investors have to pay for this growth. To me, this is a bridge too far, and it certainly justifies the Sell rating I have on the company.

data.title: First Eagle Global Income Builder Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Shaking off the second quarter’s volatility, equity markets marched steadily higher throughout the third.

Though risk and uncertainty remained in ample supply during the summer, stocks continued to rebound off their April “Liberation Day” troughs as investors cheered continued artificial intelligence (AI) spending and the prospect of Federal Reserve easing. For the quarter, the S&P 500 Index gained 8.1%, led to new record highs by the market’s tech-oriented growth names. The MSCI EAFE Index returned 4.8% in the third quarter but maintains a year-to-date advantage of more than 1,000 basis points over US stocks. Likewise, US fixed income markets have also outperformed global issues in the third quarter, but have lagged year to date. The Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index returned 0.6% and 7.9% for the third quarter and year to date, respectively, compared to 2.0% and 6.1% for the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index.1

Market Valuations Suggest Lofty Expectations, Particularly in the US

At the risk of sounding like a broken record, we’ll again point out that risk perception in the US markets appears quite low. Equity market valuation multiples are rich, high yield spreads are tight, implied volatility is low, and the price ratio of growth to value stocks is near its all-time high.2

In short, we think it’s fair to characterize the appetite for risk in the US as full—which is not to say market prices have no foundation in reality. Earnings growth forecasts remain constructive, driven by factors ranging from the impact of the AI capex cycle to fairly accommodative fiscal conditions. In terms of the latter, the rate of growth in government spending and the size of its debt continue to exceed wage growth, imparting some positive nominal drift to the economy that has trickled down into expectations for corporate earnings and margins expectations. At the same time, short-term interest rates have moved lower following September’s widely expected 25 basis point reduction in the federal funds rate, and markets are pricing in an additional 50 basis points of cuts by year-end.3

Similarly constructive dynamics have emerged in non-US economies, beckoning the return of animal spirits to markets sorely in need of a boost and helping to drive the relative outperformance on non-US equity markets year to date. In Europe, for example, Germany has taken steps to leverage its ample fiscal capacity, notably on defense and infrastructure projects,4 and NATO countries as a whole agreed to raise annual defense spending to 5% of GDP by 2035.5 China’s multiyear debt-restructuring initiative for local governments— earmarked at 12 trillion yuan—appears to be bearing fruit, enabling local authorities to clear arrears to suppliers rather than debt service.6 In Japan, the country’s likely next—and first female—prime minister has a reputation as a pro-spending conservative who also favors stimulative monetary policy.7

Considerable weakness in the dollar over the course of 2025—the ICE US Dollar Index is down nearly 10% year to date—has further supported the outperformance of non-US equity markets. Still, it wasn’t until this year that the MSCI EAFE Index broke through its 2007 peak.8 The MSCI EAFE is trading around 17x trailing earnings—not cheap but well within the realm of normal, from our perspective, and certainly less rich than the S&P 500’s 28x.9 And while it has pulled back marginally, the price ratio of US equities to non-US stocks stands at more than 2.5 times the average since 1970.10

Unusually, Gold and Equities Have Surged in Tandem

An interesting feature of the financial markets over the past several quarters has been the concurrent rally in equities and gold prices. Gold’s year-to-date gain of nearly 50% puts it on an annual pace not seen in nearly 50 years, as central banks and investors alike have piled into the metal amid elevated risks and the potential for currency debasement. More recently, we’ve also seen other precious metals—including silver and platinum—break out to the upside.11

Historically, equities and gold have both participated in the nominal drift of the global economy, but they typically have done so in a countercyclical manner, as gold has tended to thrive in conditions less supportive of equity investment. There have been exceptions, however, perhaps most notably during the early 1970s. This period was characterized by the fiscal pressures of the Vietnam War, the end of the Bretton Woods gold peg and executive branch pressure on the Fed to ease interest rates despite inflation pressures, a combination of factors that contributed to monetary disequilibrium and a decade of stagflation.

While we don’t want to overstate the historical analogies, there are some evident parallels between that period and the current environment, including the fact that the US appears to be a long way from home base in terms of monetary and fiscal settings. For example, we wouldn’t expect the federal government to be running at a substantial fiscal deficit over the past several years—including just shy of 6.0% of GDP in the recently completed fiscal year—with sub-4.5% unemployment levels.12 Meanwhile, Trump is pressuring the Fed to lower the federal funds rate while the fiscal imbalance remains unaddressed and geopolitical tail risks have only grown fatter.

Tending the Garden

Strong markets can create a dilemma for portfolio managers; while you want to let your roses bloom, you must remain true to your investment discipline. Viewing our portfolios from the bottom up, we have looked for opportunities to recycle capital, trimming more successful positions to take advantage of other opportunities in sectors or geographies that have been out of favor.

Of course, the world remains rife with risk, including massive sovereign debt loads, geopolitical frictions and political strife. While an increase in the market’s perception of these risks would likely impact both US and international stocks, international markets in general appear to be priced with less expectational risk compared to the US, given current valuations. Regardless, we expect our investment discipline to support our efforts to provide long-term real returns while avoiding the permanent impairment of capital.

Portfolio Review

Global Income Builder Fund A Shares (FEBAX)(without sales charge*) posted a return of 5.08% in the third quarter 2025. As of September 30, 2025, the Fund’s equity allocation was 63.86%, comprising 41.78% international stocks and 22.08% US stocks. The Fund’s 20.77% bond allocation included 15.17% in investment grade issues. The Global Income Builder Fund outperformed the composite index in the period.

Leading equity contributors in the First Eagle Global Income Builder Fund this quarter included gold bullion, Jardine Matheson Holdings Limited (OTCPK:JMHLY) and British American Tobacco plc (BTI). Fixed income contributors included Mexico Remittances, 12.5%, due 10/15/2031; Government of Brazil 10.0%, due 1/1/2027; and Government of the United States of America 1.25%, due 4/15/2028.

The gold price continued to set new record nominal highs during the quarter, propelled by a rare convergence of monetary policy uncertainty, attacks on Federal Reserve independence, disruptive trade policy, shifting fiscal policies, heightened local and geopolitical tensions, and high sovereign debt levels.

Shares of Hong Kong’s Jardine Matheson Holdings rebounded during the quarter along with a broader recovery for many Chinese-listed equities. More specific to Jardine, the company’s disciplined focus on capital allocation boosted the performance of its publicly traded subsidiaries. Even after these gains, the discount at the holding company level remains significant.

British American Tobacco (BAT) is the world’s second-largest tobacco company. Higher pricing offset lower volumes for US combustibles during the recent quarter, and BAT’s alternative products were strong. The company’s modern oral product Velo—with an already strong, established presence in Europe—won traction and gained share in the US. Additionally, the hold of BAT’s brand VUSE in the US vapor market could be strengthened as the Trump administration pressures the import of illegal vape products, primarily from China. Supported by its portfolio of strong brands, BAT has a history of returning cash to shareholders through dividends and buybacks.

The Mexico remittances bonds referenced above are secured by a first lien on the remittance cash flow from the US to Mexico through the money-transfer business of Nueva Elektra del Milenio, the retail subsidiary of Mexican conglomerate Grupo Elektra. Despite recent shifts in US immigration policy, a steeper yield curve bolstered these bonds during the quarter. Remittance volumes were buoyed by the favorable exchange rate on repatriations converted from the weaker US dollar into the stronger Mexican peso.

Brazil’s shorter-duration sovereign bonds were bolstered during the quarter by stronger local currency and demand for government debt. The Banco Central do Brasil paused interest rate hikes during July—retaining a still-restrictive 15% rate—which is attractive relative to other emerging and developed market debt on both a nominal and real basis.

Shorter-dated US government debt performed well as short-term interest rates moved lower following September’s widely expected 25 basis point reduction in the federal funds rate. Markets are pricing in an additional 50 basis points of cuts by year end.

Equity detractors in the quarter included Philip Morris International Inc. (PM), Shimano Inc. (OTCPK:SHMDF) and Fuchs SE (OTCPK:FUPBY) preferred registered shares. Nearly all fixed income positions contributed to performance during the quarter given favorable market conditions; the lowest contributions came from Nufarm Australia Ltd. 5.0%, due 1/27/2030; Mileage Plus Holdings LLC 6.5%, due 6/20/2027; and AMN Healthcare Inc. 6.5%, due 1/15/2031.

Tobacco company Philip Morris reported better-than-expected earnings for its most recent quarter, but slightly soft sales weighed on the stock. The company attributed the sales weakness to supply issues in Indonesia and Turkey due to regulatory changes. However, its noncombustible products continue to lead growth. We remain constructive on this cash flow-generative business and are pleased with Philip Morris’s commitment to returning cash to shareholders through reliable dividends and stock repurchases.

Japan’s Shimano, which manufacturers bicycle parts, fishing components, and rowing equipment, lowered its forward guidance during the quarter because of weakness in overseas markets and ongoing inventory adjustments. Our investment thesis remains intact, as we are confident that Shimano can work through accumulated inventories after the strong but unsustainable demand for its products during the Covid-19 era. In addition to high-quality products and dominant global market share, the company has a strong history of returning capital to investors.

Germany based-FUCHS is a global manufacturer and distributor of high-value-add industrial lubricants for a range of applications. The company reported weaker-than-expected results for its most recent quarter due to the impact of tariffs, low industrial output in Europe and geopolitical tensions. We believe these challenges are cyclical in nature, and we continue to like the company’s ability to generate cash flow and grow its business. With concentrated family ownership and a limited free float that restricts buybacks, management has consistently returned capital to shareholders through dividends.

Nufarm (OTCPK:NUFMF) produces agricultural pesticides and develops seed technologies. Continued high leverage, weak prices for Omega-3 canola oil and the potential outcome of a strategic review of Nufarm’s seed technologies business triggered a negative ratings watch from Fitch during the quarter. We continue to anticipate resilient cash flows from Nufarm’s diversified portfolio of crop-protection products and its broad geographic reach.

MileagePlus Holdings owns the assets of United Airlines’ loyalty program and generates revenue by selling miles to partners including banks, credit card companies and United Airlines itself. The bonds referenced above were prepaid during the quarter. Although prepayments reflect the issuer’s financial strength, they tend to drive the market price of an impacted bonds toward the call price.

AMN Healthcare (AMN) provides permanent and temporary staffing—nurses, physicians and allied health professionals—for a wide range of environments including hospitals, clinics, dialysis centers and home health agencies, Performance was pressured during the quarter by operating results, forward guidance and a non-cash charge. We acquired these new-issue bonds—with a higher rate and longer term than the debt they replace—in September, and we believe they enhance AMN’s ability to support its long-term growth objectives.

We appreciate your confidence and thank you for your support. Sincerely,

First Eagle Investments

Risks

All investments involve the risk of loss of principal.

Bonds are also subject to credit risk, in which the bond issuer may fail to pay interest and principal in a timely manner, or that negative perception of the issuer’s ability to make such payments may cause the price of that bond to decline.

Bank loans are often less liquid than other types of debt instruments. High yield securities involve greater risk than higher rated securities and portfolios that invest in them may be subject to greater levels of credit and liquidity risk than portfolios that do not. Definitions

Federal funds rate is the interest rate at which depository institutions (banks and credit unions) lend reserve balances to other depository institutions overnight on an uncollateralized basis. Gross domestic product (GDP) measures the total value of all economic output in goods and services for an economy. Currency debasement refers to a reduction in a currency’s purchasing power.

MSCI EAFE Index (Net) measures the performance of large and midcap equities across developed markets countries around the world excluding the US and Canada. A net-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income net of withholding taxes. MSCI World Index (Net) measures the performance of large and midcap equities across developed markets countries. A net-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income net of withholding taxes. Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index (Gross/Total) measures the performance of the investment grade, US dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market in the US, including Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, fixed-rate agency MBS (agency fixed-rate and hybrid ARM passthroughs), ABS, and CMBS. A total-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income. Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index (Gross/Total) measures the performance of investment grade debt from local currency markets worldwide. The multi-currency benchmark includes treasury, government-related, corporate and securitized fixed rate bonds from both developed and emerging markets. A total-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income. US Dollar Index is a geometrically averaged calculation of six currencies weighted against the US dollar maintained by ICE Futures US. S&P 500 Index (Gross/Total) measures the performance of 500 of the top companies in the leading industries of the US economy and is widely recognized as a proxy for the US market as a whole. A total-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income. Nikkei 225 is a price-weighted index composed of 225 stocks in the Prime Market of the Tokyo Stock Exchange. It is widely recognized as a proxy for the Japanese equity market as a whole. German DAX® Index measures the performance of the 40 largest companies listed on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange that fulfil certain minimum quality and profitability requirements. It is widely recognized as a proxy for the German equity market as a whole. CAC 40® Index is a free-float market capitalization-weighted index that measures the performance of the 40 largest and most actively traded shares listed on Euronext Paris.

Indexes are unmanaged and do not incur management fees or other operating expenses. One cannot invest directly in an index.

data.title: Nvidia: The Bulls Strengthen The Bear Case - 2 Graphs To Make You Think Twice
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation's (NVDA) Q3 fiscal 2026 results have come in above expectations, of course. Was there any question that they would? The problem is not with the way revenues or earnings are growing now - that would be an unsupportable argument. Then why am I reiterating a Strong Sell position in the face of a deluge of positive articles on Nvidia?

To be very fair, even the recent price slide in NVDA stock is NOT a sign that this opportunity is souring, although it is a symptomatic indication of investor uncertainty, as I shall discuss later. I have always held the view that this is an excellent company with second-to-none management that thinks decades ahead of its time - and its peers. Indeed, this is what I said in my first Strong Sell article in March 2025:

My concerns are not around future growth or its ability to generate strong earnings and shareholder returns. This is a superbly run company at the helm of a singular “paradigmal” shift in the way we interact with computers. It is Nvidia that is enabling AI models like DeepSeek, for that matter. It is a disruptor, not a “disruptee.”

So, what is my bear case based on, then? Fair question, so as an introduction to this thesis extension, I shall first outline the reasons I have presented in my previous coverage of this dominant chipmaker in the AI space, and then we take the step into deeper waters as I bring up a highly contentious point of view at the end. Please be patient, and please read until the end to appreciate what I am trying to get at.

My Bear Case Explained: 2 Graphs to Make You Think

I called the recent share price decline a symptom of investor uncertainty because that is exactly what I think it is - investors without deep conviction in Nvidia's ongoing dominance in the data center GPU space jumping ship when the news cycle is negative.

This is not an opinion, mind you. When one represents this in visual form, it would look something like this:

The Timeline:

CES Disappointment - Jan 7 - NVDA "tumbled more than 6%."

DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) Announcement - Jan 27 - NVDA "shares tumbled 17%"... "erasing $589 billion from the company’s market capitalization"... "eclipsed the previous record — a 9% drop in September that wiped out about $279 billion in value — and was the biggest in US stock-market history."

Weak Guidance for Q3 - Aug 28 - NVDA "shares drop nearly 3% after chipmaking giant projects weak revenue forecast."

Alphabet-Meta Talks on TPU Deal - Nov 25 - NVDA "extends slide to drop 6% on Tuesday."

That is hundreds of billions of dollars in market capitalization wiped out each time there was bad news - or, at least, news that was perceived as being bad for Nvidia.

Cumulatively, just these four events alone have contributed to over 30 percentage points worth of decline in NVDA. Please note that these percentages may not match my graph exactly due to the declines being "moment in time" figures, but even an approximation to about 30 percent or so is not something one can ignore.

This is the sell-the-news cohort of retail investors jumping ship. The opposing force to this bull-bear group of investors (one that includes speculators and short-term traders) is the bull cohort. I shall elaborate later on why this group tends to misrepresent the opportunity with NVDA. I promise to try and avoid stepping on any toes, but I refuse to call a spade by any other name.

To make sure this analysis is not skewed by any bias, I also proffer the other side of the coin - price movements on positive news and earnings catalysts, again indexed to a baseline of 100, going up from there.

As is clearly evident, the cumulative losses on four negative news items were well over 2x the cumulative gains on four positive news reports. One can tell from the way the indexed value moved from 100 down to well below 75 on negative news versus how much it moved from 100 up to about 111.

Regardless of how one "feels" about the company and its performance on the operational side as well as on the price side, there is no denying that negative catalysts have a greater impact than positive catalysts.

That is my first bear argument, and I hold much the same view of Apple (AAPL), on which I am actually bullish, but for different reasons. I outline this in my recent coverage.

Taking that a step further, Apple is far less susceptible to what I shall call "news-based pullbacks." It is most certainly subject to a high amount of risk, but the big difference is that Apple is only exposed to a major disruption event that will affect all smartphone makers; in stark contrast, Nvidia's focus on data center GPUs for AI workloads, its current dominant position in this space (first image below), and the fact that 89.2% (second image below, nine-month figures) percent of its revenue comes from that segment make it far more vulnerable to disruption.

This is the first major risk of investing in NVDA, as I see it.

The second one, which I outlined in my September 2025 article, is the fact that these revenue inflows are highly concentrated to the mega-caps engaged in building out their AI data center footprints.

To summarize, the two greatest risk catalysts are a disruption in its current dominant position, and the fact that the bulk of its revenues come from a small pool of customers (61 percent), albeit all of them with very deep pockets.

For the third quarter of fiscal year 2026, four direct customers with sales greater than 10% of total revenue included: Customer A at 22%, Customer B at 15%, Customer C at 13%, and Customer D at 11%, which were attributable to the Compute & Networking segment.

The average retail investor may not be fully cognizant of this, but this is what gives NVDA its inherent volatility. It is a constant tussle between the bulls and the bears. At any given point in time, one side is likely to be controlling share price movements - during negative news cycles, the bears and bull-bears have the upper hand, as they did recently when news broke on the Meta (META) being in talks with Alphabet (GOOG), and during positive ones like the OpenAI (OPENAI) investment announcement, it is the bulls and bull-bears that have it. Bull-bears might make a pretty penny either way, but this is akin to scalping in my view. It is not investing.

Beyond Q3, What Makes Nvidia Investible - Or Not?

We have seen the best of Nvidia this past quarter. With quarterly revenue growing to $57 billion, up 62 percent year-on-year, and earnings per share expanding by 66 percent, one would expect the current price to correspond to prolonged performance at these levels.

However, when you look at forward multiples for sales and net profit, it looks as though much more is priced in, and this is what I cannot bring myself to digest because investing in NVDA is no longer about the opportunity but one of intense price action based on investor sentiment and highly susceptible to the news cycle - how high can this go before something sends it tumbling back down, as so many things have in the past?

One might argue that NVDA is fairly priced at a PEG ratio (forward) of 1.03. Others might opine that a price to forward sales multiple of 20x is far too much. Still others might posit that 41x cash flows is a reasonable level for a growth company at this scale?

With all these differing views, how is one to decide whether this is a golden opportunity or a bubble in the making?

Some food for thought before I step into a very contentious topic: What comes to mind when one thinks of Nvidia? Most would cite its data center GPU dominance, its high product price, handsome margins, its moat-like CUDA ecosystem, its gigantic market cap, and several other well-known positives.

But when it comes to negatives, the case is very hard to build. Believe me, I have been doing it for the past several months.

That being said, when you de-noise the investment evangelism and only look at your own position as an investor, one thing should be clear. And this is where I shall have to watch for stepped-on toes, but I shall grit my teeth and say it anyway:

Beware the Early NVDA Investor!

You will see this in every comment thread on every Nvidia article, whether it is a bullish one or a rare bearish one. The bullish comments are generally from investors who have a much lower cost basis compared to the current price. This is largely anecdotal and subjective, I know, but I think we can agree that most of the older bulls are in this cohort, and the proof is that their comments usually include an "I am up by..." component. Other parts of their comments might include article links, newly announced deals or catalysts, and so on.

The reality, however, is that an investment is only as good as the acquisition price or cost basis, and the net after-tax return on that investment. The older bulls invariably have much low cost bases, and congratulations to them because they were early birds who recognized the brilliance of Nvidia's management team; specifically, its CEO. The rest of it was luck and timing, but that is never under one's control, is it?

What remains is the fact that if you invest now, when the stock is overvalued on most metrics, your potential IRR may or may not be as good in the long run.

For example, if you had bought your shares in March 2024 in the mid-$90s, you will have had to wait a full year to see the price go like a rollercoaster and land at the same level in March 2025.

I shall not ignore the obvious and deny that the price appreciation since then has been phenomenal. But that is exactly what it is; a phenomenon, and one that is based on the balance being more often in favor of the bulls than the bears. For now.

At the extreme end of the bull spectrum, there is talk about a possible $20 trillion valuation for NVDA by 2030, even on Seeking Alpha. Last year, Forbes carried a piece based on a "$10 trillion by 2030" prediction by the same analyst.

Is one expected to believe that Nvidia will account for between 28 percent and 56 percent of America's GDP by then?

Alright, I shall even go so far as to admit that NVDA could indeed be a $10 trillion or $20 trillion company by 2030, but is that where I want the bulk of my money invested when I know the downside risks?

The main reason I still consider NVDA a Strong Sell is that, at some point, it stops being an investment and bleeds into betting territory.

I will tell you something nobody else probably will - you may be invested in NVDA just because you are afraid of being left out. That is absolutely fine if this is just one small component of your total portfolio, but what happens when NVDA keeps growing faster than other components and becomes an increasingly bigger percentage?

This is exactly why I am retaining my Strong Sell rating on this stock. It is a wonderful company with excellent growth metrics and a strong runway, but much of that is dependent on the horizon being completely free of negative news. All it would take is one single move from China to break Nvidia's supply chain for advanced nodes. Not that I think they will, and I do not want to stray into the geopolitical realm in this article, but I find it important to highlight the reality of the risk here.

I reiterate - it is not that NVDA is a bad investment. Not at all. If you have money to spare, by all means pump it into the stock - buy the dips, buy the rumors, buy the news releases, buy the earnings announcements, buy the whatever.

BUT, if your NVDA shares now represents a big chunk of your portfolio, you should be constantly trimming it down and reinvesting your gains in safer assets. Pay the tax man if you must, but get your money out so the next bad cycle of bad news does not leave you gaping into a hole where your portfolio value was.

I have this to say to newer investors with no money yet in NVDA: Do not listen to the bullish noise surrounding the stock. In fact, do not even listen to bearish noises that tell of erosion from the competition, unsustainability of valuation metrics, and what not.

What I DO want you to listen to is what your portfolio needs at this time. Do you want your money to ride on a stallion that gets spooked at every sound? He looks beautiful and runs like the wind on steroids, and he is far ahead of the pack, but would you put all or most of your money on him if you knew that one little stone in its path could send him toppling at his current speed? I would not, unless I were betting with money I can afford to lose.

The real question to you is this: are you comfortable holding a single, highly risk-sensitive asset that makes up a large-enough portion of your portfolio that you will suffer a major financial setback if things turn sour?

I do not presume to tell you where to invest your money. Let me be unequivocally clear about that. All I am doing is asking you to think reasonably about this opportunity that everyone and their grandmother is chasing.

My readers know that risk consideration is paramount in all my articles. If risk management is your primary goal as well, there are high-yielding vehicles with far safer risk profiles that you can consider. Investing is not meant to be exciting or exhilarating. It needs patience, it needs resolve, and it needs more than a little common sense. That is all I am saying.

data.title: Dentalcorp: I Will Tender Into The Acquisition Offer
data.description:
Introduction

Dentalcorp Holdings Ltd. (DNTL:CA) (OTCPK:DNTCF) owns the largest network of dental practices in Canada and is one of North America’s fastest-growing companies in the sector. I have been following the company for a few years as I was charmed by its free cash flow profile, but I also had some worries about the very aggressive growth pattern. This has put the balance sheet under pressure and that’s why I thought it was a good idea to grow at a slower pace while keeping the balance sheet safe.

In my most recent article on Dentalcorp, which was published this summer, I liked the free cash flow profile. But I wasn’t the only one who liked the improving profile as Dentalcorp attracted an acquisition offer by a private equity group. The all-cash offer will be voted on next month, and it is likely a ‘done deal’.

The Q3 results were fine but not shocking

As the Q3 results were pretty much in line with expectations, there is no real reason why Dentalcorp’s shareholders should vote against the proposal.

During the third quarter, Dentalcorp reported a total revenue of approximately C$420M, on which it generated about C$211M in gross profit. The latter represents a 13% increase compared to the same quarter of last year. Unfortunately, the G&A expenses increased pretty sharply, partially offset by lower depreciation and amortization expenses.

Meanwhile, the net finance costs came in slightly lower, but this still wasn’t sufficient for Dentalcorp to record a positive net income. The net loss of C$4.9M represented a loss of approximately 2 Canadian dollar cents per share.

That being said, the sustaining capex is substantially lower than the depreciation and amortization expenses, and my entire investment thesis was based on the cash flow statement. That likely also attracted the private equity group, as the cash flows remain strong. As you can see below, the reported operating cash flow was C$190.4M and after deducting the changes in the working capital as well as the C$21.1M in lease payments, the adjusted operating cash flow was just over C$135M.

While that wasn’t sufficient to cover the combination of cash-funded acquisitions and consideration payments (C$130M) and the C$46M in capex, the sustaining capex was substantially lower.

The C$130M in M&A-related cash outflow is, of course, by definition a growth expenditure. The image below indicates the total amount of sustaining capex was just C$18M in the first nine months of the year, which means the underlying sustaining free cash flow was approximately C$117M.

Divided over the 199.5M shares that are currently outstanding, the underlying sustaining free cash flow was approximately C$0.59 per share in the first nine months of the year.

This likely means the buyout offer will go ahead

At the end of September, Dentalcorp entered into a definitive agreement with a private equity group (GTCR LLC) whereby the latter plans to acquire all outstanding shares of Dentalcorp at an all-cash bid of C$11 per share. This values the equity at C$2.2B and the enterprise value at approximately C$3.3B. The purchase price of C$11/share represented a 33% premium to the closing price right before the announcement as well as the 20-day VWAP.

The premium was fair but definitely not a knockout bid. But as the bid was made two months ago and no higher bidder has emerged, I’d say it's pretty much a ‘done deal’.

The acquisition price of C$11/share represents a premium of 31% and 75% versus the share price in my two most recent articles on Dentalcorp, so I have no reason to be disappointed. Additionally, the C$11/share represents an attractive multiple versus the sustaining free cash flow result, so there is no real reason for me to object to the deal.

Investment thesis

The Q3 results were fine but definitely not shocking, so there is no real reason not to accept the buyout offer. I will tender my shares into the C$11 cash bid, and although I still feel the share price could have run higher in the future, the time value is important to me, and cashing in C$11/share right now is likely more valuable than, for instance, seeing C$13/share in 2027.

Dentalcorp is a ‘hold’ and a ‘tender’ right now. And I’m looking forward to redeploying the incoming cash in other ideas.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AMD: Downgrading Back To Sell, Horrible Crack In Momentum (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
My last Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) rating change in April here, was an upgrade to Hold, with an optimistic slant. The valuation and chart setup had become far more enticing than 6-12 months preceding that decision. My mild bullishness on AMD stood in stark contrast to other Big Tech names, which I would/will not touch on the long side, because of clearly unsustainable valuations. [The only multi-trillion company I have a bullish rating on is Alphabet-Google (GOOGL, GOOG), which I explained in March here.]

Since that effort, AMD's share quote rocketed from $86 to $267 in October, on the back of AI-chip demand optimism. While some sort of rebound was justified, I am getting very concerned the valuation has run too far again on the upside into unsustainably extended territory.

Even worse news, several momentum indicators, including the 14-day Ease of Movement creation (which I have talked about in past AMD articles for several years), has plummeted to new all-time record lows in November. It's so much of a slap in the face, I feel I have no choice but to downgrade Advanced Micro Devices back to Sell. Let me explain my thinking with some charts, comparing the current technical setup to historical trading patterns.

Topping Pattern Redux?

Really, the last two major AMD price peaks in November 2021 and January 2024 had eerily similar momentum trading patterns to October-November 2025. If both of the previous setups successfully screamed to get out, why would today's situation be any different?

The standout problem in November 2025 is the massive implosion in the 14-day Ease of Movement indicator. All-time lows under 58 (boxed in gold below) in recent days are screaming a vacuum of buying interest may now exist. The indicator looks at how much volume is necessary to move price. In essence, very little volume has dropped the stock by -20% over a couple of weeks. It appears a price top and reversal is underway like past instances when new all-time lows were reached in November 2021 and March 2024.

Other momentum concerns include an October reversal in the Accumulation/Distribution Line (circled in blue) and a top in the Negative Volume Index in August (circled in green). ADL looks at where price closes each day inside the daily trading range, multiplied by volume. NVI only counts buying/selling trends on weaker volume days (when less news and noise exists to attract outside capital). Flat to poor performance in both has been present at the last two important peaks for the share quote.

2024 Peak

The previous price top, followed by a better than -50% drop was outlined in March 2024. Coincidentally or not, the 14-day EMV reached new all-time lows (at that time) in April and again in May. I actually wrote a bearish article here explaining this rotten momentum pattern for AMD.

A reversal in the ADL (circled in blue) and weak performance in the NVI indicator were also part of the technical picture in March and early April 2024.

Investors and traders would have kept a large portion of their capital intact had they sold and reentered AMD at a later time (into April 2025) or just avoided buying the stock over the next 6-12 months.

2021 Peak

The November 2021 to January 2022 period was the next period of topping with a momentum pattern similar to today. 14-day EMV reached new all-time lows in December and again in January, warning investors that aggressive selling was overwhelming buying interest.

ADL reversed all the way back in August 2021, while NVI was fading for months. The end result was a -60% drop in price, peak to trough, into October 2022.

My worry (and analysis summary) is history does rhyme. So, the wicked crack in upside momentum the last 3-4 weeks should not be discounted off hand. We could be entering another extended span of price decline in AMD.

Overvaluation Problem Is Here & Now

Justifiable by AI-chip sales growth or not, today's valuation of the stock on underlying operating metrics is above the peak readings of the original Dotcom technology boom in 1999-2000.

Price to sales and cash flow are perhaps the most straightforward way to compare the valuation over many decades (as free cash flow and earnings have fluctuated wildly year to year at AMD). On the graph below back to 1999, you can visualize this circumstance.

Another view of reality is based on the enterprise valuation, including debt and equity totals, minus cash holdings. On EV to core EBITDA and sales, AMD is equally trading near the highest end of its valuation spectrum. What I am saying is the stock may now be "priced for perfection" above $200. Nothing can go wrong from today's ultra-bullish expectations, or the share quote will tumble. Interestingly, the share valuation in November 2025 mirrors the topping periods of late 2021 and early 2024.

Seeking Alpha lists AMD at "D+" for an overall Quant Valuation Grade. Sounds bad enough if you want some underlying value on trailing numbers for your stock portfolio. However, this is compared to a wildly overvalued semiconductor sector today. If you compare AMD to the S&P 500-typical financial ratio setup in November 2025, it would easily score an "F" grade. 57x trailing cash earnings and 112x GAAP EPS has no long-term value to me (buying many regular businesses outside of tech with equivalent multiples under 10x in recent weeks). The upside arguments are entirely dependent on rare and incredible operating business growth rates projected in 2026-27.

Final Thoughts

I am just not willing to own a stock that is counting on extraordinary growth to justify its valuation. Still, if the AI-boom does continue for a number of years, AMD investors buying in the low $200s may earn some money. However, the odds of a wipeout in price are extraordinarily high, assuming the global economy slows and AI-chip competition heats up.

You can see how Nvidia (NVDA) has reacted negatively in trading over the last week on the news Meta Platforms (META) is being courted to buy Google AI chips for some of its datacenter expansion. Competition is going to increase exponentially in GPU and TPU chip design and pricing, a function of Nvidia's profitability motivation.

It's quite possible sales growth at AMD will be spectacular for 3-5 years, while earnings and free cash flow remain lacking. That's how capitalism and free-market capitalism works, especially for the cyclical semiconductor industry. The idea is consumers/businesses and users of chip technology could benefit smartly from the AI revolution, perhaps at greater rates than datacenter infrastructure stocks like AMD.

In this vein, current estimates for growth (especially for EPS) are now a truly optimistic view of future results, in my humble opinion. If we get a recession next year that slashes technology CAPEX spend by companies of all shapes and sizes, current estimates for AMD sales and earnings will prove overly bullish. AI-datacenter buildouts could be scaled back or cancelled, and new AI-chip entrants could force AMD to slash pricing to keep business.

If you continue to hold Advanced Micro Devices, you have to be betting on a Goldilocks economy of low/falling inflation and interest rates, plus a growing economy with steadily increasing demand for AI-chips.

I do not have confidence such a scenario will play out next year. Either a recession is coming soon, or far higher interest/inflation rates will materialize over time. As such, the risk/reward setup seems completely skewed against sizable gains in AMD during early 2026, at a minimum.

I would rather buy and hold stocks with greater upfront value, that actually will benefit from a recession (or can at least survive one) in terms of investment capital flows, looking at past historical occurrences in trading.

With the chart readout screaming to get out of AMD, I feel I must downgrade my 12-month outlook of the stock to Sell again. Investors that bought around $100 early in the year have experienced a stupendous winning streak. There's no disgrace in capturing large trading profits. At the very least, please consider selling covered calls (or buying out-of-the-money puts) on your position to hedge any significant downturn in price.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: 8 'Safer' Dividend Buys In Barron's 23 Better November Bets Than T-Bills
data.description:
Foreword

While most of this collection of Barron’s Better Bets (BBB) is too pricey, or reveals somewhat skinny dividends, eight of the fifteen highest yield Dogs with the “Safest” dividends of the BBB are ready to buy. November finds Conagra (CAG), Altria Group (MO), Verizon (VZ), Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), Kimco Realty (KIM), UDR Inc (UDR), KeyCorp (KEY), and Regions Financial (RF), as the buyable top eight, living-up to the dogcatcher ideal. That is, each showed an annual dividend from $1K invested exceeding its single share price.

The remaining seven of the November “Safer” fifteen have prices exceeding the dividend returned from $1k invested. Of the seven, ONEOK (OKE) is closest to the mark with a price within $6.70 of the interest paid from $1k invested.

With renewed downside market pressure of 49.33%, it will be possible for all ten of the top BBB “Safer” dividend dogs to become elite fair-priced with their annual yield (from $1K invested) meeting or exceeding their single share prices.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced November BBB Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and the aforementioned nine as “safer” choices in the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 18.44% To 29.95% Net Gains From Top-Ten BBB Dogs By November 2026

Six of ten top BBB Dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top-ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this November, 2025 yield-based forecast for BBB dogs, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 60% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to November 2026 were:

ONEOK was projected to net $299.54 based on the median of target price estimates from 20 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 6% over the market as a whole.

LyondellBasell (LYB) was projected to net $251.01, based on the median of target price estimates from 20 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 12% less than the market as a whole.

Verizon was projected to net $228.24, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 65% under the market as a whole.

Kimco Realty was projected to net $215.34, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 25 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 28% over the market as a whole.

Kinder Morgan (KMI) was projected to net $210.59, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 21 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 23% under the market as a whole.

Conagra Brands was projected to net $207.59, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 17 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 97% under the market as a whole.

Equity Residential (EQR) was projected to net $207.29 based on the median of target prices estimated by 26 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 10% less than the market as a whole.

KeyCorp was projected to net $205.02, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 23 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 18% more than the market as a whole.

Philip Morris International (PM) was projected to net $191.83, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 18 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 57% less than the market as a whole.

Pfizer (PFE) was projected to net $184.40, based on the median of target price estimates from 26 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 45% less than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 22.01% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten BBB stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 26% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

Top ten November BBB Dogs occupied seven of eleven Morningstar sectors.

In first place, was the lone basic materials dog, LyondellBasell [1]. Then, two consumer staple stalwarts, placed second, and third, Conagra Brands [2], and Altria Group [3].

Thereafter, a lone communication services sector member placed fourth, Verizon [4], followed by two health care sector members in fifth and eighth, Pfizer [5], and Bristol-Myers Squibb [8].

Finally, another loner from the consumer discretionary sector placed tenth, Best Buy, (BBY) [10], and completed the November Dogs of the BBB by yield.

One of 23 November, 2025 BBB Placed in the Top Ten by Yield, Target, and Return

ONEOK Inc in the energy sector was the only November BBB member to place in the top tn in all three scoring categories.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

BBB Prices per Dividend Dollar

The least expensive dog of the BBB is LyondellBasell at $8.58 per dividend dollar and the most expensive is Entergy at $37.21 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Five of Twenty-Three BBB Dogs Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Five BBB so infected are: LyondellBasell, Federal Realty, Kinder Morgan, Williams Companies, and Entergy. These are considered unsafe to buy. Furthermore their infection explains how the are missing from the IDEAL “safer” dividend dog reckoning.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, tariffs, trade-offs, war-games, rants, and uncertainty.

Price Drops, Dividend Increases or Dividend Splits Could Get All Ten BBB Dogs Back to "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, eight in the top ten are already ideally priced. Two more are in striking distance of Ideal.

Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts, just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 13.69% To 25.14% Top Ten BBB Upsides To November, 2025; (32) No Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 15.43% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 BBB Dogs As of November 25, 2026

Ten-top BBB dogs were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten BBB dogs selected 11/25/25 by yield, represented seven of eleven sectors. (Financial, Industrials, Technology, and Utilities, missed the top-ten cut.)

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten BBB Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 15.43% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. Five higher-priced Dow top-yield dogs as of November 25 were: Bristol-Myers Squibb; Realty Income; Altria; ONEOK; Best Buy, with prices ranging from $49.05 to $79.66.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As winter blows in and the market shivers, the feckless Fed-fueled slide and slithering presidential trade pronouncements add momentum into 2026 (with the exception of a few overly speculative and meme stocks) look for all of the top ten of twenty-three BBB Dogs to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or real quick!
