data.title: SBIO: A Shock Biotech Winner In 2025
data.description:
I don’t think a lot of investors were predicting this would be the year in which small-cap biotech stocks absolutely crushed it, and yet that’s what we’ve seen. The ALPS Medical Breakthroughs ETF (SBIO) has made one of the market’s more improbable comebacks after a long and often painful downward spiral. It reached a low in early April and exploded higher in the subsequent months, contrary to the general small-cap weakness, as well as Healthcare (until a broader sector rebound recently).

The natural question to ask is whether this recovery represents a real change in the biotech terrain, or just another fleeting upswing in a sector that has long been known for its booms and busts. Small-cap biotech lives in a difficult space: It feeds off scientific progress, but it survives on market risk sentiment. Many companies have no revenue, no profits, and no visibility beyond their next capital raise. In a market struggling with high interest rates and tightening financial conditions, the strength of SBIO’s rally is remarkable and maybe even counterintuitive. That makes it worth asking: What has changed, what hasn’t, and whether investors should see the comeback as a chance to capitalize or a reason to worry.

Why Biotech Rebounded Despite Higher Rates

SBIO’s move from its April lows coincided with an environment where the broader macro backdrop seemed far from friendly. The Federal Reserve had kept policy tight, borrowing costs for unprofitable companies were high, and investors had plenty of safer options that paid respectable yields. In ordinary times, that kind of mix is a headwind for biotech. These companies rely on outside capital to carry promising drug candidates through lengthy testing. And when money is pricey, the sector typically suffers.

But something broke the pattern this year. Some of the move is just a sign of how far biotech had plunged in the first place. By early spring, many small-cap names had already begun pricing in nasty outcomes. A few also traded below the cash they had on hand, effectively indicating that the market viewed their pipelines as having no value. Once sentiment gets this negative, it doesn’t take much for it to change.

What may have happened is investors started paying attention to drug approvals, which slowly began to rebound. After a lull, the FDA started approving an accelerating pace of new drugs, providing investors with reassurance that science was still progressing even as stock market losses mounted underneath the selloff. There was also more positive clinical news. Even places that investors had given up on, such as gene therapy, put out fresh data suggesting early concerns may have been unfounded.

Then came the deal activity. Big pharmaceutical companies are approaching a patent cliff in the second half of this decade, and their requirement to replenish their pipelines is pressing. After stock prices improved and their coffers filled with cash, they charged back into the market as aggressive buyers. That in itself shifts the biotech landscape. Each acquisition not only raises the stock of the target company, but also reboots expectations for all players in the group. When the giants are in a shopping mood, investors feel safer taking risks in the smaller innovators.

The impact is felt more by SBIO than most diversified biotech ETFs due to the product’s makeup. The fund targets companies that have trials in their final stages and at least two years of available cash—the very ones that acquirers usually go after. With deal flow accelerating, some of SBIO’s holdings were acquired outright, and others rose on takeover speculation. The fund’s design started to be rewarded by the market, and its relative performance improved as a consequence.

Add that all up—better industry conditions, improving clinical news, M&A, and even the first in a decade of Fed rate cuts that would be modest enough not to spell recession—and biotech suddenly had itself a good narrative strong enough to break correlation with the broad small-cap universe. That decoupling alone implied that something more elemental was also happening below the surface.

Why the Skeptics Are Still Cautious

For all the optimism, biotech is still biotech: volatile, sentiment-driven, and heavily exposed to the cost of capital. Much of the sector remains unprofitable. Most firms will need additional financing down the road. If the Federal Reserve slows or pauses its easing cycle, or if inflation forces policymakers to re-tighten, biotech would once again find itself operating in an environment where money is scarce and expensive.

There is also the issue of expectations. A rapid rebound can create its own risks if prices begin to reflect flawless execution. Deal activity has been strong, but it is unlikely to remain this intense indefinitely. Pharma companies are disciplined buyers; they will not chase valuations if they start to drift too far ahead of fundamentals. If traders begin to price in a takeover premium across the entire sector, disappointment becomes easier to trigger.

Regulatory and political risks also deserve attention. Drug pricing has become a recurring priority in Washington, and it often resurfaces at the least convenient times. Any pressure on pharmaceutical revenues naturally feeds back into the perceived value of early-stage drug pipelines. The same applies to shifts in global risk sentiment. When markets turn defensive, biotech is among the first areas investors abandon.

Finally, part of the 2025 rally may have been amplified by short covering. Several hedge funds had been betting against small-cap biotech earlier in the year. As prices turned higher, those positions became painful and were forced to unwind. Short covering can produce sharp gains, but it does not guarantee long-term buying interest once the squeeze is over.

A Balanced View for Advisors and Investors

The simplest conclusion is that SBIO’s rebound is supported by more than pure hype. The quality of the underlying companies is better than it was during the pandemic bubble. Scientific progress is visible. The industry’s weakest players have largely been flushed out. And large drugmakers are validating the sector’s value through acquisitions.

Yet none of that eliminates the risks. Biotech remains one of the most rate-sensitive pockets of the market. It will always behave like a high-beta trade on liquidity, sentiment, and macro confidence. For long-term investors or advisors, the prudent approach is to acknowledge both sides. There is a legitimate case for exposure, but not without position sizing, risk controls, and clear expectations.

data.title: Nvidia: Just Like Cisco In 2000
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig.

Investment Thesis

While I have been bearish on shares of Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), I will admit, stock in the GPU giant has had impressive, long-term success. The company’s shares have booked YTD growth of more than 32%, ~30% over the past year, and more than 1,200% over the past five years.

However, after peaking at just over $207 per share in late October, Nvidia stock has experienced a bit of a pullback over the last month (especially after earnings). Shares are now sitting below $180 per share, as of the time of this writing.

Nvidia is undoubtedly the most important company in today’s AI industry, as it has a disproportionate impact on the overall US stock market.

I think (and as Michael Burry pointed out earlier this week) Nvidia’s current vendor financing mechanics mean the company has similar operational issues as Internet router giant Cisco Systems (CSCO) did in 2000 during the dot-com bubble.

Nvidia and the AI boom in 2025 is strikingly similar to Cisco and the Internet boom in 2000. I think this is a bearish outlook for the GPU giant.

Cisco once believed that its growth curve was practically unlimited. This turned out to not be the case.

Will the same thing happen with Nvidia?

I’m not convinced that the AI boom will last forever, and I fear that we could see the same thing play out with Nvidia that we saw with Cisco.

For now, and despite an overall strong earnings report, shares remain a strong sell.

Why I'm Doing Follow Up Coverage

I once worked with an economist who taught me the following:

The stock market is one of the few leading economic indicators for where an industry or an economy is heading. Most economic indicators (CPI data, Jobs reports, etc.) are inherently delayed data. The market is not. It is forward-looking based on the collective voting opinions of millions of investors, each with their own viewpoints and access to key information.

The point with his lesson is that the stock market can often start to price in business changes well before the core operating company notices anything on its bottom line.

There are a variety of reasons why I am doing follow up coverage on Nvidia. Before the company reported earnings last week, I wrote an earnings preview, detailing where the company was at prior to its report. While the company beat earnings estimates, I think this economist’s lesson is becoming clear.

In the last two earnings cycles, Nvidia management has continued to push how AI adoption is taking over and how the company is going to dominate in this AI cycle. As I'll discuss later on in this article, these statements very closely align with Cisco’s language that was used in 2000 during the dot-com bubble.

To be clear, much like how Cisco survived the dot-com bubble and is now a hugely profitable tech giant, I am not saying that Nvidia faces existential risk.

What I am saying is that I think there is downside left in the stock and the market has not priced it in.

This week, Nvidia’s investor relations team made a rare point to critique the work of a Substack author (Dr. Burry). They did this with a note to sell side analysts on Wall Street. It's incredibly rare for the world's largest company to make an effort to critique the research of one writer on Substack.

I think this only adds to my point: the Nvidia-Cisco analogy is now becoming mainstream. This is why I am doing follow up coverage.

Nvidia's Story Is Like Cisco's Story

Before I dive more into the similarities, I must address the fact that no company today is exactly the same as what we saw from Cisco in the early 2000s. Technology has evolved dramatically, and cycles simply tend to differ, especially after a period of time as lengthy as a quarter of a century.

In essence, history does not repeat itself, but it does rhyme.

With this, there are structural similarities between Nvidia today and Cisco from 25 years ago.

During the peaks for both companies, each tech giant discussed how their respective technologies are inevitable.

In 2000, Cisco published an annual report that said the following:

The Internet is changing everything, including every idea of what is possible.

Cisco also boldly proclaimed in the report that, “in the near future, a business not on the Internet will not be in business.”

While this bold proclamation proved to be spot on for the most part, this took well over a decade to manifest.

Cisco’s then CEO John Chambers was also quoted saying the following, according to Wired back in 1999:

The internet revolution will determine which companies survive and which get left behind.

These were not cautious statements. Instead, they were bold proclamations, declaring that the internet would change the way in which people view and interact with the world.

To be clear, I think what Cisco said was 100% spot on with enough time. And, I think a lot of what Nvidia is saying will also be spot on with enough time.

I am arguing that the market is pricing in Nvidia’s predictions too soon.

Today, Nvidia is using some of the same commentary, including some of the following quotes:

​​The transition to Accelerated Computing is foundational and necessary...

AI is now infrastructure, and this infrastructure, just like the internet, just like electricity, needs factories.

We Have Only Begun To Scratch The Surface Of AI’s True Innovative Power.

These companies are strikingly similar in the sense that they are both in the center of their respective industries. Cisco was the backbone of the internet infrastructure build-out in 2000, with a good majority of spending toward the internet flowing through Cisco’s routers.

Fast-forward to 2025, Nvidia now sits in the same position for AI datacenter infrastructure. Basically, every hyperscaler and every AI-related company relies heavily on Nvidia’s silicon, systems and software stack.

The big problem that I see here is that the market (both then and now) assumes that the build-out will last decades, and that growth rates will remain high the entire time. However, the opposite tends to be true. Instead, stock prices tend to drop before the supercycles tend to slow.

In this case, let's look at Q3 earnings for Nvidia this past week.

Nvidia's top-line revenues of $57.01 billion came in higher than estimates by $1.91 billion. On the EPS front, profits came in $0.10 cents per share higher than estimates when looking at EPS estimates from a GAAP standpoint.

In fact, overall revenues grew 62.9% YoY.

Last November (November 2024), JPMorgan did a fantastic job analyzing Cisco’s EPS vs. stock price growth during the dot-com bubble.

In this case, what’s key about the dot-com bubble (for us to learn from) is that stock prices for key technology companies can peak months (if not a whole year) before EPS or forward EPS estimates peak.

In our case, for Nvidia, forward earnings estimates continue to increase for the forward 12-month basis. This still means we can be at (or close to the top) of the bubble.

Valuation

Nvidia is priced as if AI is not just a revolution, but a force that will drive increasing, straight line up demand for decades to come with only marginal volatility in year-to-year orders. I just simply think this will not be the case. Every technological revolution appears unstoppable at the peak of its respective infrastructure cycle, and this case is no different.

Cisco also had strong revenue growth, strong gross margins, highly specialized technology and an expanding addressable market. And still, the stock collapsed. Not because the internet failed (we all know quite the opposite is, in fact, true), but because growth slowed and fell short of unrealistic expectations.

Currently, Nvidia has an overall valuation grade of an F, according to Seeking Alpha Quant. Nvidia has a forward Non-GAAP P/E grade of a D+, and it’s sitting more than 63% higher than the sector median at this point in time with a forward P/E of 37.96.

Additionally, the company’s forward EV/Sales ratio is a D-, and its ratio of 20.04 is more than 500% higher than the sector median of 3.33.

These premiums are simply far too unrealistic, in my opinion, and it only sets the stock up for failure down the road.

As I mentioned in my last piece of research, I think shares could move down another 40% from here. This would put their forward P/E at 22.77 which would be right about at the median for a forward P/E.

Bull Thesis

I think one of the biggest bull cases for Nvidia could simply be, “so what?”

So what that its chart, EPS growth, and industry dynamics line up with what Cisco had in 2000, this time is actually different and this time the growth in GPU purchases is actually sustainable (as compared to routers and networking equipment in 2000).

On the surface, this bull thesis even appears to have merit. If we look at the other key graph from the JPMorgan equity research from November of 2024, we’ll see that Nvidia’s EPS growth has been much stronger than Cisco’s was.

Here’s why I think the 2000 comparison still has merit: much like in 2000, there is too much infrastructure being built out that will not be able to generate and ROI.

In 2000, there was a surplus of fiber optic networks that led to the bubble popping on the infrastructure side.

This time, while the short-term demand for datacenters (full of GPUs) remains extremely strong, it's important to know that many of these datacenters cannot be hooked up to the grid due to power shortages in many parts of the U.S.

I talked about this in my last piece of research on Nvidia, but datacenters in some parts of the country have to wait years to get hooked up to the grid due to power shortages.

These power shortages are not ending anytime soon. What this means is that billions of dollars of capex will go into projects that can’t get turned on (and they can’t make money) for years.

At this junction, what’s the point of buying more GPUs? Why spend billions on capex for GPUs that will sit in a warehouse, lose value due to age, and not be able to generate returns.

This is why history does not repeat, but it does rhyme.

Bottom Line

To be clear, as I have said the entire time, Nvidia makes an incredible product. But, this incredible product is only as good as the return it can generate for the customers that purchase it.

Even after what appears to be a strong quarter, Nvidia still faces the same existential issues. Now, more than ever, it closely resembles the dot-com boom and bust.

I do not think Nvidia is a bad company, unethical management or poor business practices. I simply think its valuation has gotten ahead of itself given where the market is.

History has a lot it can teach us.

With this, NVDA shares are still a Strong Sell.

data.title: Nickel Miners News For The Month Of November 2025
data.description:
Welcome to the nickel miners news for November.

The past month saw nickel prices lower and LME inventory slightly higher.

Nickel price news

As of November 27, the nickel spot price was USD 14,811/t, lower from USD 15,250/tonne last month.

Nickel inventory at the London Metals Exchange [LME] was slightly higher the past month at 254,520 tonnes (251,640 tonnes last month).

Nickel spot price 10 year chart - Current price = USD 14,811/t (source)

Nickel demand v supply chart

S&P Global Market Intelligence forecast nickel oversupply to 2027, then a 'primary' nickel deficit starting in 2028 (as of June 2024) (source)

Carbon Credits forecasts a nickel oversupply to 2030, then growing deficits from 2030-2035 (as of May 2025) (source)

Trend Investing v IEA demand forecast for EV metals (IEA)

Lithium & Nickel are the two 'high importance to energy' critical materials in the medium term (2025-2035) (source - page 29)

Nickel Market News

In November Fastmarkets reported:

Nickel: Prices remain under pressure amid an oversupplied market... Indonesia key to restoring market balance...Indonesia is now implementing annual mine quota reviews–which could be used to limit supply–and has increased royalties. There is also greater environmental oversight in the country. However, these are merely supply risks since no concrete measures have been taken to limit domestic production significantly. Until concrete measures are announced, the price is unlikely to find support.

On November 22 Crux Investor reported:

Indonesia's nickel supply & the global price slump: What the market sell-off signals for investors.

  • Nickel has fallen to $14,550/t, near its four-year low, as Indonesia's refinery buildout floods the market.
  • LME inventories surged by 90,000 tonnes in 2025, reinforcing a persistent oversupply and weakening near-term price momentum.
  • Market skepticism persists despite Indonesia's planned 120–150Mt quota cuts; investors doubt the reductions will materially tighten supply.
  • Structural demand from EV batteries provides long-term support, but muted stainless steel demand continues to weigh on global prices.

Nickel Company News

Producers

Norilsk Nickel ("Nornickel")

On November 6 Nornickel announced:

Nornickel launches serial production of caissons for the first furnace of its own design...The cost of the machine is about 28 million rubles, but on the caissons alone, the company will save 340 million rubles.

On November 7 Nornickel announced: "Nornickel upgrades catalyst in Sulphur program."

Vale SA (NYSE:VALE)

Vale Voisey's Bay Mine is a key Canadian and global source of nickel. Vale plans a nickel sulphate refinery at Bécancour in Québec to supply GM with battery grade nickel sulfate with deliveries targeted to commence in H2 2026, subject to various regulatory approvals.

On November 25 Vale announced: "Vale Day 2025." Held in London on Dec. 2.

Glencore [HK:805] [LSE:GLEN] (GLCNF)

On October 29 Glencore announced:

Third Quarter 2025 Production Report...Adjusting for 5,000 tonnes of Koniambo production in the base period (prior to its transition to care and maintenance), own sourced nickel production of 52,400 tonnes was 4,900 tonnes (9%) lower than the comparable 2024 period, reflecting reductions at both INO and Murrin Murrin.

BHP Group [ASX:BHP] (NYSE:BHP)

BHP's Nickel West operations include the Mt Keith Nickel Mine in Australia. Nickel West produced 81.6 kt of nickel in the '24 financial year. In 2024 BHP temporarily suspended Western Australia Nickel operations due to low nickel prices. Stage 1 production of the Kwinana Nickel Refinery is aimed to be 100ktpa nickel sulphate.

On November 24 BHP announced:

Following preliminary discussions with the Board of Anglo American plc (Anglo American), BHP Group Ltd (BHP) confirms that it is no longer considering a combination of the two companies.

Anglo American [LSX:AAL] (OTCPK:AAUKF) - Planned merger with Teck to form Anglo Teck plc

Anglo American's sale of their nickel business to MMG Singapore Resources continue to target completion by late 2025.

On November 10 Anglo American announced: "Anglo American publishes shareholder circular for merger of equals with Teck."

Eramet [FRA:ERA] (OTCPK:ERMAY)

On October 30 Eramet announced:

IGO Limited [ASX:IGO] (OTC:IIDDY)

Jinchuan Group (owns ~60% of Jinchuan Group International Resources [HKEX:2362])

On November 21 Jinchuan Group announced: "Jinchuan Group Nickel Salts Co wins three national nonferrous metals brand honors."

Sumitomo Metal Mining Co. (OTCPK:SMMYY) ("SMM")

On November 10 Market Screener reported: "Sumitomo Metal Mining's Fiscal H1 profit jumps 16%." Full results are here. Q2 dividend is JPY65.00 per share.

On November 17 SMM announced: "FY2025 Business Strategy Briefing (November)."

Nickel 28 Capital Corp. [TSXV:NKL] [GR:3JC]

Nickel 28 holds an 8.56% JV interest in Ramu which is operated by the Metallurgical Corporation of China (“MCC”).

data.title: Standex International: Shares Are Too High For My Liking
data.description:
No investor, not even Warren Buffett, is perfect. I certainly am not. Even though I have 17 years of experience in investing, with 12 of those years being professional experience, I certainly have shortcomings. And if I eventually grow to have 50 years of experience, I will still have some shortcomings. That's just reality. One of my shortcomings is that I have a tendency to be overly conservative about opportunities. I sometimes put too much emphasis on valuation and not enough emphasis on growth potential. The good news is that this doesn't usually result in me losing money, so much as it does result in me missing out on upside.

A good example of this can be seen by looking at Standex International Corporation (SXI), a business that produces things like magnetic power conversion components that are used in security products, appliances, medical products, and more. It offers up custom textures and surface finishes that can be applied to tooling, and it also offers up a wide array of other offerings like components that go into rocket engines, fuel tanks, and a lot more. Honestly, an entire article could be written dedicated to the specific products that the company provides. But that's not what you're here to read.

Well, back in June of last year, I made the decision to downgrade the company from a ‘buy’ to a ‘hold’. During the time that I had been bullish about the firm, the stock had risen 27.2%, comfortably outperforming the 18% rise that the S&P 500 enjoyed. But because of the firm's valuation, I ended up downgrading it. Since then, however, the stock has continued its market beating performance, rising 39.9% while the market is up 25.9%. In some respects, the company is looking pretty decent. But as things stand, I still cannot upgrade it, even if that means missing out on some additional upside. And that's because, quite frankly, shares are looking rather pricey right now.

Taking A Fresh Look At Standex International

Lately, financial performance for Standex International has been incredibly strong. In the chart above, for instance, you can see revenue, profits, and cash flows, for the 2024 and 2025 fiscal years. Meanwhile, in the chart below, you can see results for the first quarter of 2026 compared to the same time of 2025. What we see here is revenue, profits, and cash flows, that are growing nicely. But to really understand what's all going on, we need to dig deeper.

The fact of the matter is that Standex International, it's a rather complex business with a lot going on under the hood. There are four different operating segments that the business has, each of which deserves individualized attention. The first and largest of these is its Electronics segment, which from 2024 to 2025 reported a rise in revenue from $322 million to $400.1 million. This jump in sales came in spite of the fact that organic revenue dropped by $26.8 million because of market softness in Europe and North America. Especially problematic ended up being transportation, utilities, and certain industrial applications. It's only because of the acquisitions that the company made that revenue managed to rise.

In October of last year, for instance, the company purchased Amran/Narayan Group in a deal valued at $467.5 million. That was a massive purchase aimed at generating robust revenue in the company’s US and India-based production of low voltage and medium voltage instrument transformers. There were other purchases as well, but all of the rest of them were in the tens of millions of dollars or lower. Then again, for a company with a market capitalization today of $2.84 billion, it's a little surprising that it made a purchase as large as what Amran/Narayan Group was. As revenue for this segment increased, profitability grew as well, climbing from $64 million to $87.9 million.

This year, things are looking great as well. Revenue for the company jumped from $77.7 million last year to $110.6 million this year. Once again, organic sales for the company worsened, but this time by only $2.4 million. And according to management, this was only because of the timing of backlog conversion in its magnetics business in North America. All of the sales increase, then, stemmed from the aforementioned acquisitions that management had made. And the good news is that these acquisitions didn't bring in just additional revenue. They brought in higher profits as well, with a rise in operating income from $17 million to $28.3 million.

This is not the only part of the company that has achieved growth lately. The Engineering Technologies segment reported a rise in revenue from $83.5 million in 2024 to $102.6 million in 2025. Management chalked this expansion up to the firm's purchase of McStarlite, a provider of complex sheet metal aerospace components that management acquired in February of 2025 in a deal valued at $57 million. This added $11.6 million in revenue for the company during that time, while organic sales grew by $5.8 million thanks to stronger demand in the space and aviation end markets. I'm not surprised to see continued growth in the space and aviation space. In other articles, like this one and this one, I highlighted the potential that both of these markets have, especially following strong demand after the COVID-19 pandemic. And then, thanks to these same factors, revenue continued growing, hitting $29.9 million in the first quarter of 2026 compared to the $20.5 million reported a year earlier.

The rise in revenue the segment enjoyed did not, unfortunately, bring about higher profitability. Even though profits were up from $15.2 million in 2024 to $15.4 million in 2025, for the first quarter of 2026, the $3.6 million generated by the unit fell short of the $4 million reported a year earlier. But the good news is that this was because of purchase accounting expenses and project timing associated with the aforementioned acquisition the management closed.

The fact of the matter is that Standex International does have three additional operating segments. But financial performance from two of them has been rather mixed. The other bright spot for the firm has been its Scientific segment, which reported a rise in revenue from $68.9 million in 2024 to $72.4 million in 2025. And then, so far this year, revenue is up to $19.5 million compared to the $17.7 million reported a year earlier. Acquisition activities once again played a role here, but organic demand caused by NIH funding cuts proved problematic recently. And on the bottom line, profitability for the company has been rather disappointing, flat lining this year compared to last year while last year was down compared to the year before.

The other two operating segments are rather sizable in nature. But financial performance here has been very disappointing. In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the Engraving segment, with revenue dropping quite a bit in 2025 thanks to delays in new platform rollouts in the US and other parts of North America, while in the chart below, you can see how the Specialty Solutions segment reported similarly depressing results for 2025 compared to 2024, followed by a bit of an uptick in the first quarter of this year compared to last year.

Despite some of the troubles the company has had, this year, things are definitely looking up. Management expects, for instance, for revenue to be at least $900 million for the 2026 fiscal year. That would represent an increase of at least 13.9% compared to the same time last year. With that rise in revenue, it's likely that profitability will improve also. In fact, projecting out what we have seen already for the year, we should anticipate adjusted net profits of $105 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $91.2 million, and EBITDA of $235.6 million.

It's not just any growth that the company is going to see, though. It's growth that should be considered very attractive. According to management, revenue generated specifically from Amran/Narayan jumped 34% since the acquisition took place. Data center investments are fueling that growth, as our investments in electrification and grid modernization efforts. Management has also taken things a step further, renaming that business as Standex GRID. The expectation is that overall revenue for that enterprise should hit $270 million this year, compared to the $184 million it generated last year. On top of this, management is also working to fuel growth by launching new products. In the first quarter of this year, the company launched four such products, with plans to launch more than 15 this fiscal year. These are offerings that play in fast-growing end markets and open the company up to new product categories. In fact, new products are expected to generate $78 million this fiscal year, compared to only $55 million last year. This should create an opportunity for some nice upside in future years as well.

The only problem that I have with the company is that shares are more expensive than I would like them to be. In the chart above, you can see how the stock is valued using results from 2025 and estimates for 2026. Although the EV to EBITDA multiple for the company isn't bad, it's certainly not great. And the other trading multiples are very lofty. Relative to other similar firms, the stock is all over the map. In the table below, you can see what I mean. On a price to earnings basis and on an EV to EBITDA basis, it's around the cheapest of the group. But on a price to operating cash flow basis, it's at the very top.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Standex International 27.0 31.1 13.9
Worthington Industries (WOR) 25.8 13.2 25.7
ATS Corporation (ATS) 39.0 12.6 22.4
Kadant Inc. (KAI) 30.7 19.3 16.1
Hillenbrand (HI) 34.1 17.0 9.7
Enerpac Tool Group Corp (EPAC) 23.6 19.7 14.2

Takeaway

I would say that things are going really well with Standex International from an operational standpoint. Management is making great progress in growing the company, and clearly the market has disagreed with my assessment lately. Moving forward, I do expect further growth. But because of the firm's valuation, I'm not ready to pull the trigger on an upgrade at this time. It's possible this could mean I miss out on some upside. But is better than being wrong in the opposite direction.
